summary:
## Summary
Mike welcomed Mary to the mentorship program, shared the curriculum and short manifestos to be reviewed repeatedly, and explained that the weekly sessions will be driven by her interests and needs. He emphasized a balanced focus on deep technical skills (the vertical of the T‑shaped engineer) and broader knowledge (the horizontal). Mike introduced the use of Obsidian for daily note‑taking, demonstrated basic markdown syntax, link creation, bullet/numbered lists, and how notes interconnect via the graph view. He pointed Mary to the Obsidian Discord community for additional help and walked her through creating and navigating vaults, daily notes, and linked notes. The conversation also covered practical tools: using markdown in GitHub README files, Discord messages, and AI workflows (e.g., Whisper for audio transcription and AI summarisation). Mike shared his personal experience with AI use and mental health, stressing the importance of “AI safety” and mindful usage. He outlined the upcoming learning plan—Docker, YAML, then a deep dive into MongoDB with free training that can lead to a discounted certification exam (the cost can be covered). Mary expressed nervousness but enthusiasm, and both affirmed the plan to revisit the manifestos and continue using Obsidian throughout the week.
tldr: set up Obsidian, basic markdown, and the mentorship framework, while outlining a roadmap that includes MongoDB training and a possible certification.

summary:
## Summary
Mike spent the meeting outlining Luke’s mentorship structure and the broader goals for the MentorHub project. He introduced the T‑shaped engineer model, explaining that Luke will deepen technical skills on MentorHub while using the mentorship sessions to broaden his knowledge (the “top of the T”). Mike shared a curriculum document organized into Now, Next, Later sections and instructed Luke how to access it via Google Docs, emphasizing the “celebrated” section for completed work.  

The conversation then shifted to SRE fundamentals, the difference between DevOps and SRE, and how Google’s invention of containers (Docker) and Kubernetes reshaped IT operations. Mike described the incident‑response role, the need to support teammates (Daniel, Mary, etc.) by understanding their tech stacks, and the importance of cloud deployment on AWS.  

Practical concerns were addressed: Luke’s trouble accessing the curriculum (Google account/profile mix‑up), an Apple account recovery that’s delaying his phone setup, and the need to get a proper mouse for better navigation. Mike walked Luke through using the Cursor editor to open a local “storefront” project, import files, and experiment with generating book‑cover images via AI prompts. They also discussed upcoming tutorials (Markdown, Bash) and a plan to reconvene after a short voice‑channel session.  

Team dynamics were touched on, noting the current three‑person crew is stable for now, with a fourth hire likely focused on marketing later. Mike reinforced that lifelong learning—rather than any single language—is the core skill for a successful SRE/engineer.
tldr: Mike guided Luke through his mentorship roadmap, SRE concepts, and hands‑on tooling while sorting out access issues and planning next steps.

summary:
## Summary
Luke asked Mike for practical ways to record and transcribe meetings so he can revisit them later. Mike pointed Luke to Windows Voice Typing, shared a link to Microsoft’s support page, and explained how the Whisper AI model can be used to turn audio (e.g., from a Discord bot) into text—either locally if the PC has enough GPU power or by uploading to their Spark server. They discussed pricing tiers for Whisper, the possibility of integrating voice‑to‑text into their Mentor Hub product, and how to set up the recording workflow. The conversation then shifted to career‑related topics. Luke wanted insight into Mike’s own journey and advice for a junior developer. Mike recounted his early exposure to computers through his father’s IBM work, his diverse roles (troubleshooter at Candle and IBM, state‑government projects, consulting, and contractor positions), and highlighted that his hardest challenges were people‑related rather than technical. He emphasized the value of learning core fundamentals, building layered knowledge, and using AI tools to stay competitive. Mike advised Luke to take any first job, then gradually target “perfect” roles that match his interests, and to acquire niche skills (e.g., familiarity with a specific platform like IBM AIX) that give an edge. He also touched on work‑life balance, the impact of mental health on productivity, the importance of NPS metrics, and how large companies structure benefits and hiring pipelines.
tldr: Luke wants a reliable way to record and transcribe conversations; Mike recommends Windows voice typing, Whisper (local or via Spark), and shares career lessons about building fundamentals, leveraging AI, and targeting niche skills.

summary:
## Summary
Mike welcomes Lucky to the mentorship, clarifies that this is a separate mentor‑mentee relationship from the Mentor Hub project, and creates a curriculum document for Lucky. - He outlines the weekly meeting rhythm, the dual roadmaps (personal mentoring vs. Mentor Hub work), and the first‑week task: read two short manifestos several times and think about their meaning. - Mike introduces the “T‑shaped engineer” concept—broad, shallow knowledge across many areas plus deep expertise in one specialty—and explains why specialization matters while still being able to communicate with other specialists. - He shares his own background (long IT career, many languages, SRE focus) and stresses lifelong learning and the goal of helping Lucky secure permanent employment. - The pair discusses how to use AI coding tools effectively, including prompting strategies and avoiding over‑automation. - Mike reviews a recent miscommunication about a Python training assignment and suggests a “speed‑run” of the basics if Lucky already knows the material. - He then guides Lucky through technical setup: opening the Mentor Hub repository in VS Code (Cursor), cleaning up duplicate folders, creating a workspace that includes all relevant repos, and saving it for future use. - Mike walks Lucky through the file‑system layout (source vs. dev folders), terminal commands (cd, ls, rm), and how to navigate the workspace UI (file → add folder, collapse/expand, save workspace). - Finally, they examine the architecture diagrams (local and AWS deployment) showing the various SPAs, APIs, Docker containers, MongoDB, Cognito, Stripe, SES, MailHog, and how they map to the codebase, ensuring Lucky understands the overall system layout. - The session ends with Mike confirming that the day’s objectives are covered.
tldr: Mike walks Lucky through the mentorship onboarding, explains the T‑shaped engineer model, assigns first‑week reading, discusses AI‑assisted coding, and helps set up the Mentor Hub code workspace and review the system architecture diagrams.

summary:
## Summary
**Summary:** Mike and Mary held a mentoring session that blended technical guidance with personal check‑ins. After a brief health update (Mary’s blood‑sugar and diabetes management), Mike steered the conversation toward working on Mary’s Obsidian notes and the Cursor schema editor. They walked through adding and editing properties (e.g., email, state program), using enums, creating polymorphic lists, handling data types, rearranging fields, and managing versions, indexes, and test data. Mike emphasized manual schema construction for profile, resource, and learning‑path dictionaries before relying on Cursor automation. They also discussed workflow tips (undo, commits, source‑control) and the importance of understanding UI controls. Toward the end, the dialogue shifted to Mary’s personal challenges—housing, parole‑office logistics, a pending transfer to Tennessee, disability benefits, and transportation—where Mike offered support, outlined next steps (call Courtney for housing, confirm PO meeting, finish schema work), and encouraged continued mentorship.
tldr: Mike guided Mary through building and refining data schemas in Cursor while also supporting her health, housing, and parole‑office logistics.

summary:
## Summary
**Summary:** Luke and Mike started by chatting about lactose intolerance and food cravings, then moved to cooking tips and mac‑and‑cheese recipes. The conversation shifted to professional advice: Mike recommended posting story‑focused LinkedIn updates that link to a GitHub‑hosted portfolio rather than just showcasing projects, and he explained how to manage technical debt from dependencies. They discussed gaming development paths, highlighting JavaScript frameworks like Phaser for casual web games and Unity for full‑scale 3D titles, and suggested Luke try both. The dialogue then covered AI’s role in software and creative work—how developers use AI to speed up coding, while graphic designers may or may not adopt it, and the broader industry impact on art, music, and job security. Mike contrasted web‑app design (short, task‑focused interactions) with website design (long‑form engagement) and stressed the importance of aligning intent with UX. They examined ageism and salary expectations in hiring, the varying employment models (large enterprises, consulting firms, small custom shops, startups), and the value of senior roles like solution architects. Throughout, they exchanged personal anecdotes and reaffirmed the mentorship relationship.
tldr: Mike gave Luke practical advice on LinkedIn/GitHub portfolios, gaming career routes, AI as a tool, and navigating different software‑employment models while balancing personal interests and industry realities.

summary:
## Summary
**Summary:** - Mike introduced the benefits of a one‑to‑one microservice setup (separate SPA, API, and database) for easier debugging, independent deployment, scalability, and clearer AI context. - He contrasted this with monolithic designs, noting the higher risk of merge conflicts, slower scaling, and tighter coupling. - The conversation covered practical tooling: Lucky should reinstall MongoDB Compass for DB inspection, install the AWS CLI and authenticate daily, and use CodeArtifact as the proxy for all npm/pip dependencies, especially the shared “API utils” package. - Mike clarified how CodeArtifact version‑locks libraries, protects against external registry outages, and requires a one‑time daily AWS login. - They reviewed the mentor‑mentee workflow, emphasizing that Lucky’s curriculum is a learning path (modules → topics → resources) and that he should skim module overviews without diving too deep yet. - Lucky expressed feeling behind despite being on track; Mike reassured him that there are no hard deadlines except the weekly goal‑setting. - The agreed weekly objectives for Lucky are: revisit MongoDB (download Compass and explore), install and configure the AWS CLI, and become comfortable with the CodeArtifact workflow, while continuing to explore the Engineer Kit modules. - Mike will sync with the broader team later and keep the mentorship focused on Lucky’s career growth and the Mentor Hub project.
tldr: Mike explained to Lucky why a microservice architecture (one SPA per API) is preferred over a monolith, walked through the tools they need (MongoDB Compass, AWS CLI, CodeArtifact), and helped set concrete weekly goals for Lucky’s learning and Mentor Hu...

summary:
## Summary
Mary and Mike started by confirming they were in the right Slack channel and then moved into a one‑on‑one about the Mentor Hub project. Mary expressed nervousness about asking questions in front of the team, and Mike reassured her that asking questions is essential for learning and that no question is “stupid.” They reviewed the test‑data generation workflow, the MongoDB API tasks folder, and identified the “journey” collection as the database counterpart to Mary’s Google‑Doc curriculum. Mike walked Mary through the ERD, explaining the relationships between identity, profile, journey, resources, mentors, mentees, and the independent system tables (events, enumerators, versions). He clarified how the dashboard, subscription, and card collections will store user‑customized UI settings and payment info. Mary shared her personal background and how coding helped her after incarceration; Mike praised her progress and encouraged continued curiosity. They wrapped up with action items: Mary will explore the shipped tasks, review the journey schema, and spend a few focused hours each week on the curriculum, while Mike takes a short break before rejoining the team channel.
tldr: Mike reassured Mary that asking questions is valued, clarified the Mentor Hub data model and curriculum migration, and set clear next steps for her to explore the MongoDB tasks and journey schema.

summary:
## Summary
Mike and Daniel met for their regular mentorship session. They spent time locating and navigating Daniel’s curriculum document in Google Drive, reviewing the Agile Software Development Manifesto and the Software Craftsmanship Manifesto, and comparing their core values. Mike highlighted how both emphasize learning by doing and helping others, while noting the craftsmanship manifesto adds a focus on code quality and peer collaboration. They then moved on to the “Engineer Kit” resources, selecting “Human Experience” and “Design Fundamentals” as priority topics for Daniel to read and watch before the next meeting. The conversation shifted to practical matters: Daniel’s personal progress (job, rent, driver’s license), his difficulty logging into Discord, and the need for alternative career plans if the MentorHub internship doesn’t result in paid work. Mike encouraged Daniel, affirmed his senior‑level craft, and set the next task: sketch a profile‑detail page for the MentorHub dashboard, with a plan to discuss it in their next call.
tldr: Mike guided Daniel through curriculum navigation, manifesto comparison, resource selection, and next‑step design work while addressing personal logistics and career‑contingency planning.
